history:
$Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ had escorted the expeditionâs scientists around Port Jackson during the expedition stopover (late June-18 November 1802) and provided them with information about the areaâs natural history. He also assisted $PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$ in compiling lists of words in the Aboriginal language and supplied specimens to the visiting mineralogists. In return, LeschÃĐnault, the expeditionâs botanist, gave him plant specimens he had collected which $Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ sent to $Joseph Banks$Joseph_Banks$ in England for identification. 
 However, when in the company of the French scientists, $Paterson$William_Paterson_(explorer)$ also gathered some intelligence about the aims of the French voyage and their intentions in Australia, which he communicated to Governor King. Most disturbing to the governor was information (sourced to $PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$) that the French intended to form an establishment in Tasmania.

characteristic: French Island lie within Western Port, commonly known as Western Port Bay. Captain Hamelin, who visited the coasts of $Bass$George_Bass$ Straight aboard the Naturaliste in April 1802, suspected that the copy of the sketch made by $Bass$George_Bass$ in 1797-98 he had on board was partly âinaccurate.â He therefore sent his geographer Faure, accompanied by his officers $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and BrÃĐvedent, in both his large and small boats to carry out a survey of Western Port, instructing them to make the necessary observations to enable the Naturaliste to enter and anchor in the port, and to produce a chart of it. They discovered that âthere to the east, at the back of the basin, an island was joined to the mainland by a silt bankâ. On the other boat, BrÃĐvedent was âthe firstâ to circumnavigate it. It was named Ãle des FranÃ§ois [FranÃ§ais]. Notably, thisexploratory and reconnaissance mission occurred simultaneously with the celebrated encounter between Baudin and Flinders, while Le GÃĐographe and Le Naturaliste were navigating independently. The island is now a national park.

[10 avril 1802] nous dÃĐscendimes le soir sur le cÃītÃĐ occidentale de la 2.eme passe le capit.ne de frÃĐgate Miliu [$Milius$Pierre_Bernard_Milius$] a reconnu que cette portion de terre indiquÃĐe sur la carte anglaise comme faisant partie du continent est une ile ; il paroit quâelle nâest habitÃĐe que trÃĻs passagerement, car nous ne trouvames que quelques traces de feu, fort anciennes, et dÃĐja la vÃĐgÃĐtation avoit rÃĐparÃĐ les ravages occasionnÃĐs par les flammes, avec toute la vigueur que peut favoriser un sol excÃĐllent dont lâhumiditÃĐ est, sans cesse, entretenue par un hombrage habituel ; aussi ce ne fut quâavec difficultÃĐ que nous trouvames un petit emplacement moins embarassÃĐ dâarbustes et de lianes pour passer la nuit, elle fut assez dÃĐsagreable Ã  cause du grand nombre de mousquites qui nous assaillirent, cependant un grand feu, autour duquel nous nous couchames nous en dÃĐlivra en partie. history:
On board their small boat, $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and Faure discovered that âto the east, at the bottom of the basin [Western Port], an island was connected to the mainland by a mud bank.â From the other boat accompanying the surveying mission, BrÃĐvedent circumnavigated it, and the island was named Ãle des FranÃ§ois [French Island]. In his journal, the botanist Leschenault, who was accompanying $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$, noted the following about the island: "On the 20th of Germinal, Year X [10 April 1802], we landed in the evening on the
western side of the second channel. Lieutenant-commander $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ perceived that this portion
of land, which is marked on the English chart as forming part of the mainland, is in fact an
island. It appears to be inhabited only occasionally, as we came across just a few, very old,
traces of fire, and the vegetation had already recovered from the ravages of the flames â with
all the vigour that occurs where an excellent soil is kept damp by a perpetual cover of shade.
When we looked for a place to spend the night, it was only with difficulty that we found a
small area that was not entirely clogged with bushes and creepers. It was a rather unpleasant
night, as we were attacked by swarms of mosquitoes. We slept around a large fire, which kept
them at bay to some extent."

characteristic: Phillip Island, located at the entrance to Western Port Bay, was first examined by $George Bass$George_Bass$ during his whaleboat voyage of 1797â1798. A more systematic survey was subsequently undertaken by Lieutenant $James Grant$James_Grant_(navigator)$ in 1801. Unaware of $Grant$James_Grant_(navigator)$âs prior work, the French expedition later charted the island with the intention of improving upon $Bass$George_Bass$âs preliminary observations, and the map he had roughly drawn up. See French Island

history: The botanist $Leschenault$Jean-Baptiste_Leschenault_de_La_Tour$ notes that : âThis island is around four leagues long, from east to west, and one and a half leagues from north to south. We landed on a sandy beach, and as far as I was able to venture inland I found only sand. However, the plants grew densely and vigorously, which I attribute to the dampness of the ground and to the alluvial soil which, I believe, must lie beneath the sand. That evening we returned to the headland where I had stayed alone, and we passed the night there. We remained there for the whole of the 26th [of Germinal â 16 April 1802] while Citizens $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and Faure plotted all the hazards that lie in the main entrance to the harbour.â

history: âThe western end of this island forms the southern point of the entrance to the channel that leads to Port Western. When coming from Wilsonâs Promontory, one must round this point because of the islet I have just mentioned [Seal Rock] âĶ Then one should keep to the middle of the channel to avoid the sandbanks that extend from the shore on both starboard and port sides for about a mile; these banks are in no way dangerous because the sea always breaks over them. When coming from the south, one can also avoid rounding the said islet by passing between it and another islet shaped like a mound [Round Island] âĶ The passage is fine, and one may proceed without concern to the anchorage in the large basin âĶâ

characteristic:
.Located a short distance west of the entrance to Western Port Bay, on the Mornington Peninsula, the cape was shaped by the erosive action of salt water on basalt rocks, with geological formations that developed over millions of years in the volcanic strata (Pulpit Rock).

history:
In Napoleonic time, $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ was seen as the personality who restore the royal authority after the Wars of Religion and the assassination of the king $Henri IV$Henry_IV_of_France$, and whose policy aimed to make France a European leader. Richelieu was an important person to the Navy as he established the French Royal Navy and founded the French trading companies based on the model of the Dutch East India Company (VOC) to allow France to control and develop its trading expansion. $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ created the French Academy (lâAcadÃĐmie franÃ§aise) in 1635, that controls French language to make it a better instrument of thought.
 More generally, $Richelieu$Cardinal_Richelieu$ was acknowledged for establishing centralized administrative structures that strengthened the Stateâs power. His principles in implementing organizational reform were in phase with those later adopted by NapolÃĐon.

$Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PÃĐrigord$ fut contraint de passer plusieurs annÃĐes en exil, d'abord en Angleterre, puis aux Ãtats-Unis. Lors d'une rencontre entre NapolÃĐon et le tsar Alexandre $Ier$NapolÃĐon_Ier$, au congrÃĻs d'Erfurt en septembre 1808, $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PÃĐrigord$ joua double jeu et prit secrÃĻtement contact avec le tsar pour lui conseiller de refuser les propositions d'alliance de NapolÃĐon. De retour en France, il commenÃ§a ÃĐgalement Ã  discuter secrÃĻtement de la succession de $NapolÃĐon Ier$NapolÃĐon_Ier$, car l'empereur n'avait pas d'hÃĐritier lÃĐgitime et $Talleyrand$Charles-Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PÃĐrigord$ voulait prÃĐparer une transition pacifique.
history:
$Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PÃĐrigord$ was forced to spend some years in exÃle, first in England and then in the United States. After his recall, he was appointed Minister of External Relations during le Directoire* in 1797. Some two years later, 19 November 1799, he was one of the instigators of the coup d'ÃĐtat which saw the accession to power of $NapolÃĐon Bonaparte$Napoleon$, who appointed $Talleyrand$Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-PÃĐrigord$ to the position of Minister of External Affairs. Le plat aurait tant plu Ã  Bonaparte quâil aurait ensuite demandÃĐ quâon le lui resserve sous le nom de poulet Marengo. history: According to a well-known legend, after the Battle of Marengo, Bonaparteâfamished after the fightingâasked his cook, FranÃ§ois Claude Guignet, known as Dunand, to prepare him a meal. As the field kitchens had been damaged, the cook had to improvise with whatever could be found: chicken, olive oil, tomatoes, garlic, eggs, crayfish, and croutons. The dish was said to have pleased Bonaparte so much that he later asked for it again under the name chicken Marengo. In subsequent versions, chicken was sometimes replaced by veal, considered a more refined meat.

Cape Desaix (now Cape Otway) marks the western entrance to Bass Strait. It now lies within the Great Otway National Park, an area supporting a wide range of habitats and numerous endemic species; Cape Marengo stands at the edge of the same coastal sector.
The cape was named after General Louis Charles Antoine Desaix (1768â1800). At the moment when the French were on the verge of defeat at Marengo (see Cape Marengo), Desaixâs arrival with reinforcements and a decisive counter-attack helped reverse the course of the battle in favour of France. His intervention secured one of the most celebrated victories of the Consulate, though he himself was killed in action.

history: $Volney$Constantin_FranÃ§ois_de_ChassebÅuf,_comte_de_Volney$ became a member of the SociÃĐtÃĐ des IdÃĐologues, whose political ideal aspired to secular and anti-authoritarian reforms, guided by reason and science. characteristic:
Baudin had originally named this small projection along the western Victorian coast Cap des falaises (Cape of Cliffs) as "all the coast that we saw looked to me to be unapproachable and not even to offer shelter for a vessel..." (Baudin's journal, 30 - 31 March 1802). He was describing part of the limestone cliffs from which coastal erosion has fashioned the group of pinnacles now known as the Twelve Apostles (although only eight pinnacles remain today), a short distance further to the west.
 $Jean-Charles de Folard$Chevalier_de_Folard$, known as the Chevalier $de Folard$Chevalier_de_Folard$, (1669-1752), after whom the landmark was later named, was a war strategist and engineer who commented, annotated, and translated Polybius from Greek into French, and whose works included major references used to master the art of war. Folard advocated infantry attacks in columns rather than in lines, and his theory was widely discussed in military circles. $PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$ extends the notion of the coastâs inhospitality to include its animal life. characteristic: This is a small flat island (2 km long and 1 km wide) located a short distance off the western Victorian coast. It was formed by sub-marine volcanic eruptions between about 6 and 8 million years ago and is Australiaâs only offshore volcano. The plateau surface is bounded by cliffs on all sides. Shore platforms and reefs prevent ship landings apart from a sheltered cove on the northern side. Several seabird species and Australian fur seal use the island for breeding. $Antoine-FranÃ§ois Fourcroy$Antoine-FranÃ§ois_de_Fourcroy$ (1755-1809) submitted a thesis on the use of quinine for the treatment of intermittent fevers for his diploma of Doctor of Medicine but did not practice medicine. His best claim to scientific achievement is his collaborative work with the acclaimed chemist $Antoine Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$*, in 1782, on a new standardized chemical nomenclature. He also carried out extensive research on the analysis of minerals and demonstrated that aragonite and calcite were two forms of calcium carbonate. He initiated the method of proximate analysis [to determine the general composition of organic substances]. He was the author or co-author of many publications.

Bien que NapolÃĐon ne fÃŧt pas un humaniste, sa personnalitÃĐ nâÃĐtait pas dÃĐnuÃĐe de paradoxes, et les ÃĐcrits $de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ lâaccompagnÃĻrent tout au long de sa vie.

$PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$, lui aussi, eut ses propres contradictions. Au dÃĐbut de lâexpÃĐdition, il percevait les peuples autochtones Ã  la maniÃĻre $de Montaigne$Michel_de_Montaigne$ â comme des ÃŠtres fondamentalement humains et capables dâamitiÃĐ â, mais Ã  son retour, sa vision avait changÃĐ. history:
Montaigne displayed a remarkable openness toward other cultures. For example, despite the cultural prejudices of his time against Indigenous peoples, he went to Rouen to meet three men from Brazil who had just arrived in France and were reputed to be âcannibals.â In his Essais, Montaigne argues that Europeans âsurpass them [the Indians] in all kinds of barbarism.â Although Napoleon was not a humanist, his character was not without paradoxes, and Montaigneâs writings accompanied him throughout his life.
$PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$, too, had his own contradictions. These did not prevent him from choosing the name Montaigne for the voyageâs nomenclature, even as he moved away from Montaigneâs ideas. At the beginning of the expedition, he viewed Indigenous peoples much as Montaigne hadâas fundamentally human and capable of friendshipâbut upon his return, his outlook had changed. He no longer described those he had once called his âfriendsâ as âgood savages,â but rather as ineffective âpeoplesâ on whom he conducted dynamometer experiments to measure their physical strength.

characteristic:
The site lies at the southwestern tip of Cape Bridgewater, on a basalt peninsula in western Victoria. Investigations at the Cape $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ have revealed an early-Holocene Aboriginal coastal landscape, with a series of open-air Aboriginal shell midden deposits situated at the top of a 50 m high cliff, with the ocean currently washing against its base. The French expedition bears witness to this Aboriginal presence and their use of fire, which shaped the coastal landscapes, as indicated by the coastal profiles made aboard the GÃĐographe. $PÃĐron$FranÃ§ois_PÃĐron$, whose writing is characterized by a rhetoric that is at once emphatic, sensitive, and at times excessive, also notes that âthe terrain presents here and there a few sad and languishing shrubs,â introducing a barely perceptible nuance into the âscene of desolation and sterilityâ he ascribes to the rest of the coastline.
Cape $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ was named in honour of $Abraham Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (died in 1688), a distinguished seventeenth-century officer of the French navy.
history_fr: $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$, en collaboration avec $Jean-Baptiste Colbert$Jean-Baptiste_Colbert$, a contribuÃĐ Ã  la crÃĐation et Ã  l'institutionnalisation des grades navals dans la Marine royale. Bien que rÃĐputÃĐ pour ÃŠtre un individu peu sympathique, il a ÃĐtÃĐ cÃĐlÃĐbrÃĐ pour sa victoire dÃĐcisive sur l'amiral nÃĐerlandais Ruyter en MÃĐditerranÃĐe, un triomphe qui lui a valu d'ÃŠtre reconnu comme le plus grand capitaine franÃ§ais de son ÃĐpoque, Ã  ÃĐgalitÃĐ avec Ruyter et les plus grands commandants anglais et nÃĐerlandais.Le nom de $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ dans la nomenclature tÃĐmoigne de son utilisation comme figure exemplaire Ã  lâÃĐpoque napolÃĐonienne, incarnant le modÃĻle du grand marin que NapolÃĐon souhaitait ÃĐriger pour soutenir sa politique de reconstruction navale aprÃĻs le dÃĐsastre de Trafalgar. Dans cette perspective de restauration Ã  long terme de la marine franÃ§aise, lâun des deux navires-ÃĐcoles crÃĐÃĐs en 1810 pour assurer la formation annuelle de 300 ÃĐlÃĻves-officiers de marine reÃ§ut le nom $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (celui basÃĐ Ã  Toulon).
history: $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$, together with $Jean-Baptiste Colbert$Jean-Baptiste_Colbert$, contributed to the creation and institutionalization of naval ranks in the Royal Navy. Though reputed to be an unsympathetic individual, he was celebrated for his decisive victory over the Dutch Admiral Ruyter in the Mediterranean Seaâa triumph that earned him recognition as the greatest French sea captain of his time, on par with Ruyter and the foremost English and Dutch commanders. The name $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ in the nomenclature reflects his use as an exemplary figure during the Napoleonic era, embodying the model of the great naval commander that Napoleon sought to promote in support of his long-term policy to rebuild the French Navy after the disaster of Trafalgar. In this context of naval restoration, one of the two training ships established in 1810 to educate 300 naval cadets each year was named $Duquesne$Abraham_Duquesne$ (the one based in Toulon).

Prominent painters, such as David, contributed to this effort by exalting his grandeur, as exemplified by Davidâs famous painting $Bonaparte$Napoleon$ Crossing the Great St Bernard Pass (1801). In it, Napoleon is depicted astride a rearing steed, pointing toward the pass to be crossed â an idealised image, given that he in fact made the journey on a mule. characteristic: The Carpenter Rocks constitute a distinctive feature of the rugged limestone coastline of southeastern South Australia. Wounded in Madras in 1758, he was imprisoned by the English and later released on parole. He returned to military action and was captured a second time. After his release, he was appointed vice-admiral of the seas of Asia and America in 1777 and, despite his naval skills being questioned by his subordinates, took an active part in the American war of independence alongside the Marquis de $La Fayette$Madame_de_La_Fayette$.

history: In 1793, the Revolutionary government (La Convention Nationale) entrusted $Dombey$Joseph_Dombey$ with the mission to present in to the United States a meter in copper to Thomas Jefferson and the Congress and to promote the new metric system. He was also asked to observe the organization of the young republic, and was provided with a long and detailed questionnaire, like those given to Baudin, to review the scientific, political and economic specificities of the States. He embarked on 14 October 1793 on an American brig, the Soon of New York, under Captain Nathaniel Williams Brown. Inopportunely, privateers attacked the brig and the unfortunate $Dombey$Joseph_Dombey$ was made prisoner on the island of Montserrat (West Indies) where he died in 1794.

history:
Morard-de-Galles continued to serve in the navy following the revolution, but was imprisoned during the Reign of Terror, in 1793. The take-over of the government by the French Directorate in 1794 led to his release. In 1798, $Morard$Justin_Bonaventure_Morard_de_Galles$ commanded a French Fleet, including transports carrying 1,500 soldiers, in an unsuccessful attempt to assist the Society of the United Irishmen, a revolutionary republican organisation, in their attempt to revolt against British rule. The aim of the French was to land in Ireland in the winter of 1796-1797 to join the Irish Rebellion. Hamelin* took part in this venture.

history:
It is said (but not proven) that NapolÃĐon discovered a pair of special points (the NapolÃĐon points) at the center of a triangle, which are interconnected with the Fermat pointâthe point whose total distance from the three vertices of the triangle is minimized.
Fermat made other major mathematical contributions and is best remembered for his last theorem that states that no three positive integers a, b, and c satisfy the equation an+ bn = cn for any integer value of n greater than 2. For over three centuries, mathematicians struggled with its proof, which Fermat famously said he could not fit into the narrow margin of the page he was working on.
Remaining a conjecture for almost 300 years, Fermat's Last Theorem was finally proven in 1993 by Andrew Wiles, using a complex proof that likely relied on mathematical approaches and tools very different from those available to Fermat. An international collaborative project is now working to "teach" this proof to a computer.

history:
On the Isle de France, $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$ organized the system of national education and introduced an adapted version of the Napoleonic Civil Code, known as the Code $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$. $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$ became the personal adversary of $Matthew Flinders$Matthew_Flinders$, whom he kept under house arrest on the island from December 1803 to June 1810. At that time, France and Great Britain were competing powers in the Indian Ocean, and $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$âs principal objective was to safeguard the French colony from potential British invasion, a threat that materialized shortly after $Flinders$Matthew_Flinders$âs release.
The conditions of $Flinders$Matthew_Flinders$âs confinement, however, were from 1805 far from those of a prison. He enjoyed a notable degree of freedom and formed numerous friendships among the islandâs inhabitants, whose social and cultural life he actively shared and appreciated.
Although the British Admiralty held an unfavorable opinion of $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$, Napoleon Bonaparte regarded him as a man of moderation. In response to $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$âs defense of General Moreauâwho had been implicated in contacts with conspirators against the EmperorâNapoleon is reported to have remarked: âYou are kind, and you think that everybody is like you.â

history:
$Joseph-FranÃ§ois Dupleix$Joseph_FranÃ§ois_Dupleix$ assumed the governorship of Pondicherry in 1742. Supported by his multilingual wife, whose upbringing spanned diverse cultures, he significantly consolidated Franceâs commercial influence within the East India trade. As the War of the Austrian Successionâpitting France against Britainâextended into the Indian Ocean, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranÃ§ois_Dupleix$ enlisted the support of French military officers, notably under the command of $Bertrand$FRClaude_Bertrand_(astronome)$ FranÃ§ois MahÃĐ de La Bourdonnais, to safeguard French interests in India. Their collaboration, however, was marked by persistent disagreements, ultimately souring into a contentious relationship that contributed to $Dupleix$Joseph_FranÃ§ois_Dupleix$âs eventual financial ruin.

Nonetheless, $Dupleix$Joseph_FranÃ§ois_Dupleix$âs legacy underwent a reevaluation during the Napoleonic era, as he came to symbolize the ambitions of Franceâs colonial expansion in the East. His establishment of trading outposts and the facilitation of French access to the Indian subcontinent positioned him as a pivotal figure in NapolÃĐonâs vision of oriental imperialism, embodying the potential for French territorial and economic growth.

characteristic:
Jussieu Bay lies along a high-energy stretch of coast shaped by ocean swell, wind-driven waves, and persistent strong winds. These forces have produced extensive transgressive fields of parabolic dunes inland. The modern dunes have advanced some 2 km inland, whereas older systems, now stabilised by calcrete or vegetation, reach more than 5 km from the present shoreline.
The Jussieu dynasty occupied a leading place among the botanists of the Jardin du Roi in Paris, which became the Jardin des Plantes in 1793 and on whose grounds the National Museum of Natural History (see glossary) was established during the French Revolution. It played a pioneering role in the development of new systems of plant classification, first with Bernard de Jussieu (1699â1777) in the eighteenth century, and then with his nephew Antoine-Laurent de Jussieu (1748â1836), who gave this new classification its most influential formulation at the close of the same century.

Dans son journal nautique datÃĐ des 6-7 dÃĐcembre 1802, Freycinet consigne les circonstances de son ancrage dans le dÃĐtroit sÃĐparant les deux principales ÃŪles de lâarchipel : "Voyant que je ne pouvais pas gagner au vent avec la voilure que le vent me forÃ§ait de garder, je me suis dÃĐcidÃĐ Ã  venir mouiller dans le dÃĐtroit formÃĐ par les deux plus grandes des Iles Hunters. Mr Boullanger & moi, nous sommes rÃĐsolus Ã  ne pas quiter notre actuel mouillage, sans en avoir fixÃĐ prÃĐalablement sa position, pour des observations de latitude & de longitude." Sur la carte de lâatlas rÃĐsultant de lâexpÃĐdition, Freycinet localise avec prÃĐcision ce point dâancrage, dâoÃđ lui et Boullanger firent les relevÃĐs des ÃŪles Hunter. history:
Among the members of the expedition, FranÃ§ois PÃĐron stands out for the number of place names dedicated to him by Freycinet: six in total, a record in the nomenclature. However, the name Canal PÃĐron does not refer to any direct discoveries made by PÃĐron in this area, as he did not take part in Freycinet's mission to the Hunter Islands.
In his logbook dated 6-7 December 1802, Freycinet recorded the circumstances of his anchoring in this strait/channel separating the two main islands of the archipelago: "Seeing that I could not gain windward with the sails that the wind forced me to keep, I decided to anchor in the strait formed by the two largest of the Hunter Islands. At 3 o'clock in the evening, I dropped anchor in the northeast of the long island in 20 fathoms of sandy bottom. Mr Boullanger and I decided not to leave our current anchorage without first determining its position for latitude and longitude observations." On the map in the atlas produced as a result of the expedition, Freycinet precisely locates this anchorage, from where he and Boullanger took bearings of the Hunter Islands. These measurements served as the basis for the triangulation of the archipelago, thus contributing to its scientific cartography.

history: Baudin describes the river as "an inlet that extends deep into the country, winding endlessly in different directions." He also notes that in the vicinity... "we found two rather unusual and interesting monuments, erected by the natives of the country, although we could not ascertain the reason for this. The first was located seven or eight feet from the stream in a place devoid of grass, three feet in circumference and surrounded by well-sharpened spears painted red on the pointed side. There were eleven in all. Parallel to this trophy, on the other side of the stream, there was a similar set in terms of shape and number of spears ... Several people thought that these spears had been painted red with blood, so we took one to examine it more closely and realised that the colour had been applied using eucalyptus resin."

history: Freycinet noted the âthe distressing aridityâ of the coastal region, attributing the apparent desolation of the landscape to the fires and smoke associated with the practices of the Indigenous peoples. Alongside PÃĐron, he perceived these large-scale fires as a practice that seemed, from their European perspective, both ancient and counterproductiveâappearing to diminish vegetation, deplete soil fertility, and even, in their view, undermine the natural abundance of the land. However, their assessment reflected a limited understanding of the ecological and cultural significance of these practices. Without recognizing the potential rationale or long-term benefits of controlled burningâsuch as land management, resource renewal, or cultural traditionsâthe French explorersâ interpretations were shaped by their own agricultural and environmental frameworks. This gap in understanding contributed to a broader, and often uncharitable, perception of Australiaâs First Nations peoples, highlighting the challenges of cross-cultural encounter and the complexities of evaluating unfamiliar practices through an external lens.

The voyage, however, faced persistent logistical and operational difficulties, notably the frequent separation of the corvettes and then the Geographe and the Casuarina.which delayed progress and exacerbated tensions. Baudinâs journal reflects his frustration, attributing the inefficiencies in part to the inexperience of his scientific team. In his sea journal, on 9 March 1803, il observed that their lack of practical expertiseâparticularly in assessing coastal distances and depthsâhindered their contributions, despite their theoretical knowledge. While acknowledging their potential for improvement with experience, Baudin emphasized the critical role of hands-on training in fieldwork.

history:
The GÃĐographe was a twenty-gun corvette of the Serpente class. Launched under the name Uranie in 1797 and renamed GalatÃĐe in 1799, the vessel was not, however, immediately put afloat: her builder, having not been paid, refused to launch her until she was purchased for the purposes of the expedition. It was then that she received the name GÃĐographe. After returning to France in 1804, the ship was decommissioned in December 1807, though it continued to serve thereafter as a service vessel.
It was in this bay, on 30 and 31 May and again on 10 June 1801, that the expedition made its first landings, marking both its earliest onshore reconnaissance in the southern lands and its first recorded encounters with Aboriginal inhabitants.

Cape Bouvard is a limestone cape fringed by sandy beaches, and backed by an elongate lake (Lake Clifton).
Alexis Bouvard (1747-1843) was a French astronomer who helped Freycinet by providing him with astronomical observations he and MÃĐchain (see the entree Cap MÃĐchain in SA) had made at the observatories of Seeberg and Paris.
history_fr: Ces donnÃĐes et observations ont permis Ã  Freycinet et Boullanger (voir l'entrÃĐe Baie Boullanger en Tasmanie) de recalculer ou de corriger certaines latitudes et longitudes du voyage. Bouvard ÃĐtait issu d'un milieu modeste, mais en 1785, lorsqu'il quitta les Alpes pour s'installer Ã  Paris, il suivit les cours de mathÃĐmatiques gratuits dispensÃĐs par le CollÃĻge de France et dÃĐveloppa des compÃĐtences exceptionnelles en calcul qui lui permirent d'entrer Ã  l'Observatoire de Paris comme ÃĐtudiant astronome, puis comme astronome en 1795. Conscient du potentiel de Bouvard, Laplace lui obtint un poste au Bureau des longitudes en 1794. Il dÃĐcouvrit huit comÃĻtes et prÃĐdit l'existence d'une planÃĻte au-delÃ  de l'orbite d'Uranus, qui fut plus tard baptisÃĐe Neptune.
history: These data and observations allowed Freycinet and Boullanger (see entree Baie Boullanger in Tasm) to recalculate or correct some of the voyageâs latitudes and longitudes. Bouvard came from a poor background, but, in 1785, when he left the Alps to live in Paris, he attended the free mathematical courses offered by the CollÃĻge de France and developed great, even exceptional, computing skills that allowed him to enter the Paris Observatory as a student astronomer and then as astronomer, in 1795. Realising Bouvard's potential, Laplace arranged for him a position in the Bureau des longitudes in 1794. Oral traditions of the Noongar peoples, the Indigenous custodians of the region, include narratives of traversing to these islands during the Dreamtimeâa sacred era that encapsulates both the spiritual and physical landscapes of their ancestors. 
The French named the island after either the geographer Jean-Nicolas Buache or possibly his uncle, Philippe Buache (see the entry for Cap Buache in Tasmania).

history:
Berthollet was known for his use of chlorine and potassium as bleaching agents and for his support of $Lavoisier$Antoine_Lavoisier$âs ideas. With his friend $Monge$Gaspard Monge$, he took an active part in the establishment of the Ecole Polytechnique. Later they both met Bonaparte in Italy, who asked them to organize his Egyptian Expedition, on which an expeditionary force was accompanied by 167 scientists and artists. In 1802, Bonaparte appointed him President of the Commission responsible for drafting the encyclopaedic narrative of the voyage âThe Description of Egyptâ (11 volumes), which was possibly competing against PÃĐron and Freycinetâs narrative of the Baudin voyage.
At the time of the return of le GÃĐographe, Berthollet published a work that introduces the new notion of chemical affinities, where he set out his revolutionary ideas about how chemical affinities are not fixed but depend on physical conditions such as concentration, temperature, and the relative quantities of substances involved in a reaction.

Bailly, the mineralogist, and the midshipman Charles Moreau (1781-1808) were assigned to the party, and both played an active role during the mission. After encountering great difficulties in crossing the bar at the mouth of the river and further upstream, they advanced probably as far as Whiteman Bridge at the eastern edge of Perth. I had it supplied with several shovels and tackle and equipment necessary in the event of a grounding, and requested that Citizen Freycinet send back to me as soon as possible Citizen Faure, engineer-geographer, little accustomed to spending such miserable nights in the open air in rainy weather.â [19-20 June 1801]

Boullanger Island is a small granitic island locatedsouth of Jurien Bay on the coast of Western Australia, approximately one kilometre offshore from the mainland. It forms part of the Fisherman Islands Nature Reserve within the Jurien Bay Marine Park, a protected seascape distinguished by extensive seagrass meadows, rocky shorelines, sheltered sandy bays, and a high diversity of marine life. 
The island is of notable ecological and biogeographical importance as the sole habitat of the Boullanger Island dunnart (*Sminthopsis boullangerensis*), a small marsupial mouse currently classified as a vulnerable species. The island takes its name from the senior geographer associated with the expedition that first charted the area.

characteristic:
Mount Lesueur, Western Australia, is a prominent flat-topped mesa (tabletop mountain) located within Lesueur National Park and forming part of the Gairdner Range. Rising abruptly from an ancient lateritic plain, its stratigraphy reveals a sequence of ancient sandstones and quartzites capped by a resistant duricrust, a structure that has preserved the mesa through differential erosion while generating steep escarpments and sheer cliffs along its margins. These geological conditions have fostered a mosaic of habitats and contributed to the areaâs exceptional levels of endemism, particularly among its diverse and seasonally abundant wildflower flora.
The toponym of the mount commemorates Charles-Alexandre Lesueur (1778â1846), who was appointed official artist of the Baudin expedition following its stopover at Ãle de France (Mauritius) in MarchâApril 1801 and the withdrawal of the artists Jacques-GÃĐrard Milbert and Michel Garnier initially designated by the commission responsible for selecting the expeditionâs scientists.

PÃĐron et Lesueur ont, Ã  eux seuls, fait connaÃŪtre plus dâanimaux que tous les naturalistes voyageurs de ces derniers temps Âŧ. Cuvier ajoutait que M. Lesueur, qui assistait PÃĐron dans la collecte de ces spÃĐcimens, ÃĐtait en mesure defournir, Ã  travers ses dessins ÂŦ les renseignements les plus exacts sur leur maniÃĻre de vivre et sur leur habitation Âŧ.
Au-delÃ  de cette reconnaissance institutionnelle, la relation entre Lesueur et PÃĐron se caractÃĐrisait par une ÃĐtroite collaboration scientifique et une profonde proximitÃĐ personnelle. Compagnons de terrain et partenaires intellectuels, ils menÃĻrent conjointement leurs travaux dâobservation, de collecte et de description, au point que Lesueur fut parfois perÃ§u, au-delÃ  de son rÃīle dâartiste, comme un vÃĐritable zoologiste. 
Avant dâassumer les fonctions dâartistes officiels de lâexpÃĐdition aux cÃītÃĐs de son collÃĻgue Nicolas Petit, les deux hommes sâÃĐtaient embarquÃĐs pour le voyage en qualitÃĐ de canonniers auxiliaires. Lesueur, who assisted PÃĐron in collecting these specimens,was able, through his drawings, to provide âthe most accurate information on their modes of life and their habitats.â

Companions in the field as well as intellectual partners, they worked jointly in observation, collection, and description, to such an extent that Lesueur was sometimes regarded, beyond his role as an artist, as a zoologist in his own right. 
Before assuming the positions of the expeditionâs official artists alongside his colleague Nicolas Petit, both men had embarked on the voyage as assistant gunners. Baudin was well aware of their abilities as draftsmen and favoured their embarkation precisely for this reason.

characteristic: The landform named PÃĐron lies within Lesueur National Park in Western Australia, in a landscape shaped by ancient marine depositional environments and extensive lateritic plains. This area is dominated by a series of sandstone mesas, among which are the elevations the French named Lesueur and PÃĐron. These heights support specialised habitats that contribute to the regionâs remarkable biodiversity. The feature was named in honour of FranÃ§ois PÃĐron, zoologist of the Baudin expedition and principal editor of the voyage narrative, whose work remained closely linked to that of his friend and collaborator Charles-Alexandre Lesueur, to whom, in echo, the neighbouring relief was named.

Lesueur fut toujours mon collaborateur et mon compagnon ; ce que je mâappliquois Ã  dÃĐcrire avec soin, il le dessinoit ou le peignoit lui-mÃŠme avec cette exactitude et cette habiletÃĐ qui lui ont mÃĐritÃĐ tant dâhonorables suffrages Âŧ. Il sâadressait mÃŠme Ã  Lesueur dans ses lettres en lâappelant ÂŦ Mon cher papa Lesueur Âŧ, bien quâil eÃŧt trois ans de plus que lui, et, Ã  sa mort, il lui lÃĐgua les manuscrits du voyage.
history: PÃĐron states that âMy friend M Lesueur was always my collaborator and companion; what I described with care, he drew or painted it with the accuracy and skill that deserve much praise.â  PÃĐron even addressed Lesueur in letters as âMon cher papa Lesueurâ, despite being three years older, and, at the time of his death, bequeathed to him his manuscripts of the voyage.

characteristic: On the atlas chart, Montagne du Naturaliste is located on the mid-west coast of Western Australia and forms part of the northern sector of the historical region known as Edels Land, a toponym introduced by Dutch explorers in 1619. The identification of this feature remains uncertain, however, as the coastal zone is characterised by numerous named hills and elevations, making it difficult to determine precisely which prominence corresponds to the feature designated by the French as Montagne du Naturaliste. The name itself was bestowed in reference to the vessel Le Naturaliste, commanded by Captain Hamelin.

history: Freycinet described the geographical feature as a plateau. In 1801, the Naturaliste sailed in close proximity to the coast while searching for Le GÃĐographe; this coastal navigation enabled the crew to identify the relief during a running survey conducted from the vessel.

Nous avons poussÃĐ un assez grand nombre de bordÃĐes au large de la pointe Rouge, et nous avons trouvÃĐ un bon brassiage depuis 32 jusquâÃ  45 brasses dâeau, selon notre distance de terre."
history: Freycinet notes: "Pointe Rouge, beyond which Gantheaume Bay extends, would have merited closer examination, but we did not enter it. Vlamingh, who anchored there in January 1697, landed at several points along the coast. He praised the quality of the anchorage and the appearance of the country, although, after several excursions inland, he was not satisfied with its resources. We carried out a considerable number of tacks offshore from Pointe Rouge and found good soundings, ranging from 32 to 45 fathoms of water, depending on our distance from the shore."

history: Hamelin characterised the bay as âvast and not very deep,â noting that it was sufficiently sheltered by Pointe Rouge/Bluff Point to allow Le Naturaliste to tack effectively and regain its course (21â22 Messidor, Year IX / 10â11 July 1801). His description reflects a practical hydrographic assessment grounded in depth, exposure, and manoeuvrability rather than in scenic qualities alone. These observations were made as Le Naturaliste was conducting a coastal search for Le GÃĐographe, from which it had been separated following a severe storm in Geographe Bay, situating the bay within a broader context of navigation under uncertain conditions and ongoing maritime reconnaissance.

characteristic:
At the most northerly point of Dirk Hartog Island,Cape Inscription is the site of the earliest known landings of Europeans on the western coast of the Australian continent. The first known European landing on the west coast of Australia was by Dirk Hartog of the Dutch East India Company's ship the Eendracht at Cape Inscription on 25 October 1616.  Hartog left a pewter plate, inscribed with a record of his visit and nailed to a post left standing upright in a rock cleft on top of the cliff. Dirk Hartog's plate was recovered by Willem de Vlamingh during his voyage of discovery in 1697.  He in turn left a pewter plate inscribed with a record of his visit.  The plate left by Vlamingh was in turn found in 1801 by Hamelin's quartermaster/chief yeoman, Le Thiers,that Hamelin sent in mission in Dirk Hartog island on the 20-21 July 1801 Hemelin left the plate in place and added his own inscription on a piece of lead sheet, nailed to another post.
It was in reference to this succession of inscriptions that the French expedition subsequently designated the site Cap de lâInscription.

On the earliest chart drawn by Faure aboard the Naturaliste, the toponym initially proposed was âPointe du Retourâ (âReturn Pointâ), intended to mark the fact that, during their reconnaissance of the Bay of Chiens-Marins, Freycinet and Faure reached this northernmost point before turning back toward the corvette. Hamelin, however, opted for a designation of broader historical and memorial significance, bestowing upon the cape a name that alluded to the succession of commanders who, over the course of successive voyages, had inscribed the trace of their passage there by means of engraved inscriptions on plaques erected at the summit of the cape.

characteristic:
Situated at the north-western extremity of the Shark Bay World Heritage area, almost directly west of Carnarvon in Western Australia, Bernier Island constitutes a habitat of considerable biological value. It supports one of the few remaining populations of the banded hare-wallaby, as well as a now endangered rodent species, the djoongari, also known as the Shark Bay mouse.
The island was named in honour of the expeditionâs astronomer, Pierre-FranÃ§ois Bernier (1779â1803).

history: In his journal, he described the islandâs sandy limestone soil as ânot very suitable for vegetationâ but wrote of the sea along its shores as full of a âprodigious quantity of fishesâ. On his excursions, Ronsard kept a look-out for sources of wood to repair or reconstruct the vesselâs boats, for which he had responsibility. Baudin valued his skills, as did the Navy Minister Pierre-Alexandre-Laurent Forfait (the Minister of the Navy before Denis DecrÃĻs' appointment) who probably fostered his candidacy for the voyage. characteristic:
Situated to the west of the Peron Peninsula, Henri Freycinet Harbour, also known as Freycinet Estuary, is one of the inner gulfs of Shark Bay. The harbour is characterised by a higher density of islands than the neighbouring Hamelin Pool and, in its northern sector, by a series of narrow peninsulas locally known as 'prongs'.
Incorporated within the Shark Bay Marine Park and included in the Shark Bay World Heritage Area, the estuary has been identified as an important habitat for dugongs and forms part of a broader marine environment noted for its ecological specificity, including the presence of stromatolites. At the same time, the harbour lies within a zone that has undergone industrial modification, notably through dredging associated with the Shark Bay Salt Joint Venture.
The harbour was named by Louis de Freycinet in honour of his elder brother Henri (see also PresquâÃŪle Freycinet, Tasmania).
history_fr:
Freycinet rapporte que des bancs de sable semblent obstruer presque entiÃĻrement lâentrÃĐe du havre, dont lâindentation sâÃĐtend selon un axe sud-estânord-ouest. Il en ÃĐvalue la longueur Ã  vingt-deux lieues (environ 120 km) et la largeur Ã  deux ou trois lieues (un peu plus de 15 km). Bien que le havre ne soit pas entiÃĻrement exempt de bancs, il souligne que son bassin central comprend nÃĐanmoins de nombreux secteurs de grande profondeur, tandis que des hauts-fonds se succÃĻdent sans interruption le long des marges. La navigation y est donc souvent possible dans les eaux centrales plus profondes, mais demeure pÃĐrilleuse Ã  proximitÃĐ des rivages. Freycinet mentionne ÃĐgalement la prÃĐsence dâÃĐtangs salÃĐs le long des rivages, au nord de la presquâÃŪle PÃĐron. 
PÃĐron, en revanche, sâattarde longuement sur la prÃĐsence des dugongs dans ses descriptions du havre Henri Freycinet et du havre Hamelin. Pour expliquer lâappellation erronÃĐe de ÂŦ Shark Bay Âŧ donnÃĐe par Dampier, il observe que le navigateur anglais nâavait sous les yeux quâune tÃŠte dÃĐjÃ  partiellement dÃĐcomposÃĐe par la "digestion". Il ajoute que les deux dents allongÃĐes mentionnÃĐes par Dampier â autre source de confusion â se rencontrent Ã  la fois chez lâhippopotame et chez le dugong, ne diffÃĐrant que par leur position : portÃĐes Ã  la mÃĒchoire infÃĐrieure chez le premier, et Ã  la mÃĒchoire supÃĐrieure chez le second, PÃĐron, tome 2, p.229.
history:
Freycinet reports that sandbanks appear almost entirely to obstruct the entrance to the harbour, whose indentation extends along a south-eastânorth-west axis. He estimates its length at twenty-two leagues (approximately 120 km) and its width at two to three leagues (just over 15 km). Although the harbour is not entirely free of banks, he notes that its central basin nevertheless includes numerous areas of considerable depth, while shoals occur continuously along the margins. Navigation is therefore often possible in the deeper central waters, but remains hazardous close to the shores. Freycinet also notes the presence of salt ponds along the shoreline north of the Peron Peninsula.
PÃĐron, by contrast, dwells at length on the presence of dugongs in his descriptions of both Henri Freycinet Harbour and Hamelin Harbour. In explaining the erroneous name âShark Bayâ given by Dampier, he observes that the English navigator had only a head partially decomposed by digestion before him. He further notes that the two elongated teeth mentioned by Dampierâan additional source of confusionâare found in both the hippopotamus and the dugong, differing only in their position: in the former they are borne by the lower jaw, in the latter by the upper.

characteristic_fr:
Located in the southern part of Henri Freycinet Harbour, and measuring only about one kilometre in width by two kilometres in length, Salutation Island is today known as the habitat of the vulnerable greater stick-nest rat (Leporillus conditor, VU).
Freycinet named the island after the botanist Jean-Baptiste Leschenault de la Tour (see also Port Leschenault), but observed that âLeschenault Island âĶ is completely barren: a multitude of seabirds appear to have made their home there.â

Located in the south of Henri Freycinet harbour, measuring only about one kilometre wide by two kilometres long, Salutation Island is now the habitat of the vulnerable  species of the greater stick-nest rat Leporillus conditor (VU).

history_fr:
Tout en prenant part aux pratiques de collecte de spÃĐcimens propres Ã  lâhistoire naturelle de son temps, lesquelles impliquaient notamment la mise Ã  mort dâanimaux Ã  des fins dâÃĐtude, Leschenault se montrait nÃĐanmoins sensible au caractÃĻre dur et implacable de lâabattage menÃĐ par les chasseurs de lâexpÃĐdition, qui tuaient des kangourous dans la baie des Chiens-Marins â des animaux qui, notait-il,  nâont dâautres ennemis Ã  redouter que quelques aigles de mer, mais sous des buissons impÃĐnÃĐtrables, ils peuvent braver la voracitÃĐ de ces oiseaux, nous vinmes troubler leur retraite, et jetter au milieu deux la terreur, on en tua beaucoup."

history:
While participating in the practices of specimen collection typical of natural history at the time, including the killing of animals for study, Leschenault was nonetheless sensitised to the harsh and relentless slaughter carried out by the expeditionâs hunters, who killed kangaroos in Shark Bayâanimals that, as he observed, âhave no enemies to fear other than a few sea eagles; by taking refuge beneath impenetrable bushes, they defy the voracity of these birds. We came and brought strife and terror to their refugeâĶâ.
He further recounts that âone of the young kangaroos, which was slower than the others in leaving its place of shelter, was captured; and instead of appearing frightened, it licked the man who had just killed its mother and accepted the food offered to it. The Commander kept the animal for several months, until it died accidentally at Timor. It became perfectly tame, affectionate, and easy to feed. It ate bread and was particularly fond of sugar water.â

Lors de lâÃĐlaboration de la planche de lâatlas, Freycinet confronta ses propres esquisses cartographiques Ã  celles rÃĐalisÃĐes par Pierre Faure, gÃĐographe Ã  bord du Naturaliste. FAURE et non ses observations; ne connoissant dâailleurs par aucune donnÃĐe certaine la largeur de lâisthme Taillefer, jâai ÃĐtÃĐ rÃĐduit Ã  la supposer, et jâai conservÃĐ Ã  mes constructions lâÃĐtendue que je leur avois donnÃĐe. Ce point une fois fixÃĐ, jây ai assujetti le plan de M. history:
Louis de Freycinet honoured the three surgeons of the expedition by assigning their names to geographical features in Shark Bay: Baie Lharidon, Isthme Taillefer, and Cap Bellefin.
When preparing the atlas plate, Freycinet compared his own sketches with those produced by Pierre Faure, the geographer aboard the Naturaliste. He explains his cartographic method as follows:
âHaving only the plan drawn up by Mr FAURE, and not his observational notes, and lacking any reliable data on the width of the Taillefer Isthmus, I was obliged to make an assumption and to retain the dimensions I had adopted in my constructions. Once this point had been established, I adjusted Mr FAUREâs plan accordingly; that is to say, I rotated it about the tip of the Hauts-Fonds until the Taillefer Isthmus acquired the agreed width.â

Sediments carried into the bay bonded with these organisms and, over time, hardened into thin, layered structures, creating the fossil-like formations visible today. The pool is named after Captain Hamelin of le Naturaliste (see the entry Cape Hamelin, WA).

history: Lharidon studied medicine at the naval medical school in Brest, one of the principal training centres for surgeons and physicians in the Royal Navy. After returning to France, he published a medical thesis in 1804 a work clearly informed by his experience aboard the vessels of the Baudin expedition, on the depressive conditions of the soul, in which he argued that moral and psychological distress constituted a primary cause of scurvy. Marked by his own struggles with depression, Lharidon took his own life in 1807s.

history:
Peron recounts that during his excursion on the east coast (19 march 1803) of the Peron Peninsula in the company of Guichenault and Petit, while they were wading in the water to collect beautiful specimens of shells, a shark suddenly approached Petit. Peron writes: "Petit, in his fear, fired at the animal. Not doubting that the savages, whose recent tracks we had just seen on the shore, would hurry back at the sound, I suggested to my companions that we leave the sea immediately to retrieve our clothes and hide in the bushes. But the imprudent Mr Petit insisted, despite our pleas, on remaining in the sea, even mocking our caution. His indiscreet confidence soon gave way to terror. Indeed... we saw a group of savages who, from the top of a large dune (Cap Guichenault), were rushing towards the shore. At this sight, even Mr Petit, filled with terror, hastily grabbed some of his clothes and ran half-naked to join us..."
Petit died a few months after his return from a bad fall that resulted in a gangrene.

history:
Freycinet describes Cape Cuvier as a landform rising prominently above the surrounding terrain, appearing like âan enormous bastionâ and, like many points along this coast, distinguished by a particularly vivid red hue, visible from afar, although âno trace of vegetation can be perceived there.â He further notes that the position of the cape was established on the basis of the observations and surveys carried out by the geographer Boullanger aboard the GÃĐographe.
The attribution of the name Cuvier to this cape was most likely proposed by PÃĐron, in a context in which the surrounding waters proved especially rich in marine invertebrates. The mollusc specimens collected there provided Georges Cuvier with privileged empirical material in support of his conception of the order of the living world. The study of these organisms enabled him to demonstrate that, despite the diversity of external forms, constant anatomical structuresâsuch as shells, nervous systems, and the organisation of organsâmake possible a rigorous classification of species and a clear distinction between major zoological groups. For Cuvier, this anatomical stability constituted evidence of the immutability of species and of the existence of a fixed natural order, a conception that PÃĐron himself did not necessarily share without reservation. history: Freycinet emphasises the role of Cape Murat as a geographical boundary, defining it as the northernmost extremity of Endrachtâs Land, at once the north-western point of the continent and a point of transition toward Wittâs Land. The cape thus marks a spatial and nautical threshold, beyond which the coastline changes in character and navigational conditions become markedly more hazardous. Freycinet writes: "Cape Murat is the northernmost point of this land [Terre d'Endracht]; it is both the north-western tip of the continent and the origin of Terre de Witt. The latter, more dangerous to approach than any of the previous ones, is dotted with a multitude of islands and islets, shoals and reefs, which often hinder navigation..."

They are part of a marine protected area of the National Representative System of Marine Protected Areas (NRSMPA).They are named after Jean-Baptiste Muiron (1774-1796), one of the heroic figures of the Napoleonic wars and was aide-de-camp to Bonaparte in Italy.

history: Victor commanded a brigade in Italy in 1796, in the division commanded by $Augereau$Charles-Pierre_Augereau$. He fought in the $battle of Dego$Second_Battle_of_Dego$, before joining MassÃĐna's division. Victor subsequently captured the stronghold of Peschiera, before participating in the battles of Roveredo and Cerea, and being wounded at Saint-Georges. In January 1797, he fought at Rivoli and then distinguished himself at the siege of Mantua and at the battle of La Favorita. Promoted to gÃĐnÃĐral de division, he fought against troops of the Papal army. In 1800, he moved up to reinforce $General Lannes$Jean_Lannes$ at the battle of Montebello, and his corps contained the Austrian attack at Marengo until Lannes arrived to support him. He was made a Marshal in 1807, after the battle of Friedland (14 June 1807).

Freycinet cautiously suggests that âthe stretch of coast lying between Cape Poivre and Cape Dupuy probably belongs to the continental landmass,â while at the same time emphasising the still fragmentary nature of the reconnaissance of De Wittâs Land. history:
Freycinet writes of the group of islands he named Montebello that âthe great distance at which our vessels passed them allowed us to see them only very imperfectly. These islands are three in numberâĶâ. The three islands thus identified and shown on the atlas map of the voyage correspond to Hermite Island, Trimouille Island, and Lowendal Island.
Modern geographical knowledge shows, however, that the Montebello Islands (sometimes spelled *Monte Bello*) in fact form a vast archipelago of approximately 174 islands and islets, around thirty of which are named, located about 130 kilometres offshore from the Pilbara coast. Contemporary cartography also distinguishes the Lowendal Islands as a separate group, situated farther south, while the Montebello archipelago proper lies to the north.
This discrepancy between the initial representation and the configuration now accepted highlights both the limitations of observations made at a distance during the Baudin expedition and the gradual, cumulative nature of the cartographic understanding of Australiaâs north-west coastline.

It has been named after Adrien-Marie Legendre, (1752-1833, spelt sometimes Le Gendre) was a mathematician.

characteristic:
The Dampier Archipelago, located off the north-west coast of Western Australia, consists of a group of forty-two islands. Aboriginal populations have inhabited this region for approximately 50,000 years, and the archipelago is rich in archaeological sites, including rock art assemblages of remarkable scale and significance.
In the contemporary period, the presence of vast offshore gas and oil fields, together with nearby rich deposits of iron ore, has driven large-scale industrial development. This dynamic has transformed the port of Dampier into a major hub for the export of Australiaâs natural resources, resulting in a landscape in which industrial infrastructure and long-standing cultural heritage coexist in close proximity.
The French named the archipelago in honour of William Dampier, an English buccaneer, navigator and explorer who landed on this coast in 1699. This designation reflects the state of European knowledge at the time, based exclusively on earlier maritime accounts and unaware of the millennia-old Indigenous cultural heritage for which the region is now recognised as one of the most significant testimonies.

history:

IV, pp. 109 and 112.)

This âprodigious number of islands and isletsâ was formed between 6,000 and 8,000 years ago, when rising sea levels during a warmer interglacial period inundated coastal valleys, leaving hills and ridges exposed as islands.

Bouguer consacra une large part de sa carriÃĻre aux questions de navigation et dâarchitecture navale. Ses travaux sur les manÅuvres des navires, la conception des coques et la formulation mathÃĐmatique du mÃĐtacentre comme mesure de la stabilitÃĐ des bÃĒtiments devinrent des rÃĐfÃĐrences fondamentales de la thÃĐorie navale et de la formation des officiers. history:
Bouguer was a key figure in eighteenth-century maritime science, He was sent to Peru in 1735 with Charles-Marie de La Condamine to measure a meridian arc at the equator, contributing decisively to debates on the shape of the Earth. He published the results in La Figure de la Terre (1749).
Bouguer devoted much of his career to navigation and naval architecture. His studies of ship manoeuvring, hull design, and the mathematical formulation of the metacentre as a measure of vessel stability became foundational to naval theory and officer training. From 1745, he advised the AcadÃĐmie des sciences and the French Navy on methods for determining longitude at sea and succeeded Maupertuis as officier pour le perfectionnement de la marine. Sceptical of contemporary timekeeping technology, Bouguer opposed the use of marine chronometers for longitude determination, a stance that later came to be seen as limiting French advances in marine chronometry compared with Britain.

history: Jean Picard (1620â1682), known as lâabbÃĐ Picard, was a pioneer in the measurement of the Earthâs size. Decades before Delambre and Legendre, he applied systematic triangulation combined with telescopic instruments mounted on a quadrant to determine the length of a meridian arc between Amiens and Paris. Starting from a carefully measured baseline, Picard constructed a chain of thirteen interconnected triangles, measuring their angles from elevated points such as towers and bell towers. From the length of this meridian segment, he was able to calculate the Earthâs circumference with unprecedented accuracy.

characteristic:
Depuch Island is a rocky island separated from the mainland coast by approximately 3 km of shallow waters and tidal mudflats bordered by mangroves. The island is largely composed of angular dolerite blocks, with only limited sandy beaches along its southern and eastern shores. Sparse soils occur between rock outcrops on the summit and within deeply incised valleys. Roughly oblong in shape, Depuch Island measures about 5 km in length and 3 km in width, rising to 160 meters (Depuch Beacon).
The island holds exceptional cultural significance as a major Ngarluma Aboriginal site, containing one of the highest concentrations of rock engravings in Australia, comparable to those of the Burrup Peninsula. Depuch Island was named after the expeditionâs senior mineralogist Louis Depuch (1774â1803, see Anse Depuch, in WA).

De son cÃītÃĐ, Freycinet note : ÂŦ DâaprÃĻs les observations de M. Ronsard, lâÃŪle Depuch ne paraÃŪt pas habitÃĐe, Ã  moins que lâon ne veuille considÃĐrer comme preuve dâun sÃĐjour temporaire de lâhomme sur ces rivages la rencontre de deux troncs dâarbres brÃŧlÃĐs et de quelques laves rÃĐcemment fracturÃĐes Âŧ (p. 231).
Aujourdâhui, les ÃĐtudes gÃĐologiques identifient les roches de lâÃŪle comme de la dolÃĐrite â une roche magmatique de composition chimique identique Ã  celle du basalte, mais Ã  cristaux plus grossiers â et lâÃŪle Depuch est aujourdâhui reconnue comme un tÃĐmoignage majeur dâune prÃĐsence aborigÃĻne plurimillÃĐnaire, avec plus de 5 000 gravures rupestres recensÃĐes.

PÃĐron explained: âThe colour of this basalt,â says my interesting friend Depuch, who had several samples at his disposal, âis greyish blue; its texture is very dense, its grain fine and siliceous in appearance; small shiny blades, irregularly distributed, are scattered throughout the mass; it does not effervesce with acids and does not significantly affect the magnetic bar; its outer part has undergone a kind of alteration produced by ferruginous molecules âĶâ.
For his part, Freycinet noted: âAccording to the observations of M. Ronsard, Depuch Island does not appear to be inhabited, unless, however, one wishes to consider as proof of manâs temporary stay on these shores the encounter there of two burnt tree trunks and some freshly broken lavaâ (p. 231).
Today, geological studies identify the islandâs rocks as doleriteâan igneous rock with the same chemical composition as basalt but with larger crystal sizesâand Depuch Island is recognised for its long-standing Aboriginal presence, with more than 5,000 individual rock engravings recorded.

Ronsard (see the entry Cap Ronsard, WA) was entrusted by Baudin with the surveying of the westernmost islands of the Forestier Archipelago. However, he did not have sufficient time to examine each island in detail, and, as Freycinet notes, âRonsard Island was not visited in any particular way.â Freycinet nevertheless observes that âits shores are quite high compared to the surrounding land.â
Freycinet further cautions readers that âRonsard Island and the islets surrounding it are in a dubious position, as indicated by the letters P. D. accompanying them [on the chart of the atlas],â signalling the uncertain nature of their cartographic delineation.

history_fr:

En juin 1803, lorsque Le Naturaliste revint au Havre avec une grande partie des collections dâhistoire naturelle de lâexpÃĐdition Baudin, le sÃĐnateur Jean-Antoine Chaptal, scientifique renommÃĐ et promoteur de nouvelles mÃĐthodes agricoles â telles que lâajout de sucre au moÃŧt non fermentÃĐ afin dâaugmenter le degrÃĐ alcoolique du vin aprÃĻs fermentation (chaptalisation) â chargea Thouin de recevoir ces collections et dâen assurer la conservation. Si la majoritÃĐ des spÃĐcimens vÃĐgÃĐtaux collectÃĐs en Australie ne survÃĐcurent pas au voyage, certaines graines de plantes alors inconnues en France purent toutefois ÃŠtre mises en culture, notamment des eucalyptus et des acacias. history:
AndrÃĐ Thouin (1746â1824) was an influential and well-connected French botanist. In 1793, he was appointed to the chair of horticulture at the MusÃĐum national d'histoire naturelle in Paris. 
In the context of the revolutionary wars, some men of science, including Thouin, accepted the universality of the revolutionary ideals and that the seizure of natural history collections and rare live animals as prizes of war was permissible. When, in 1794, the French revolutionary armies occupied the Netherlands, Thouin with BarthÃĐlemy Faujas de Saint-Fond (see Piton Faujas*, Tasmania) directed the move to Paris, of Dutch natural history collections, including life elephants and stuffed animals derived from countries overseas. Later, in 1796, he was charged with a similar mission, during Bonaparte's Italian campaign. 
In June 1803, when le Naturaliste returned to Le Havre with a large part of the Baudin expeditionâs collections of natural history specimens, the senator Jean-Antoine Chaptal, a well-regarded scientist keen to develop in agriculture new methods such as the technique of adding sugar to unfermented grape to increase their alcohol level after fermentation (chaptalisation), sent Thouin to receive and take care of them. Unfortunately, most of the plant specimens collected in Australia did not survive the voyage, but some seeds from plants new to France were germinated, e.g., eucalypts and acacias. On their arrival in Paris, Thouin was ordered to send a large part of the collections, including kangaroos, cassowaries, tortoises and black swans, to the empressâs garden at La Malmaison, rather than to the Museum and its menagerie.

history:
Jacques Bedout was a Canadian-born naval officer who spent fourteenth years in the merchant navy at the beginning of his career, before joining the French Navy. 
After the French disaster of Trafalgar in 1805, Bedout ended his official career with the painful task of presiding over the Council of War to judge the officers whose conduct had been deemed reprehensible during the attack on the fleet on the Isle of Aix by English fire ships. Of the four officers involved, two were sentenced, one to death.

Ã son retour, il fut chargÃĐ par Jean-Charles de Borda de contribuer Ã  lâintroduction du systÃĻme mÃĐtrique dans les ports et arsenaux de la Marine, et joua un rÃīle important dans la normalisation des signaux cÃītiers, participant ainsi Ã  lâamÃĐlioration de la sÃĐcuritÃĐ de la navigation et des communications maritimes.
Entre 1804 et 1805, Missiessy commanda lâescadre de Rochefort durant le blocus britannique des Antilles, assurant avec succÃĻs le ravitaillement des colonies franÃ§aises et perturbant les opÃĐrations ennemies sans perdre un seul bÃĒtiment. Cette mission sâinscrivait dans une stratÃĐgie de diversion plus large liÃĐe au projet de NapolÃĐon dâinvasion de lâAngleterre, mÃŠme si lâabsence de communication complÃĻte sur les objectifs stratÃĐgiques limita la coordination avec les autres escadres franÃ§aises.
Missiessy est ÃĐgalement reconnu pour son traitÃĐ influent sur lâarmement et lâorganisation interne des bÃĒtiments de guerre, publiÃĐ en 1797. FondÃĐ sur le modÃĻle dâun vaisseau de 74 canons, cet ouvrage prÃĐconisait une standardisation des amÃĐnagements, prÃĐcisait les fonctions des diffÃĐrents espaces Ã  bord, dÃĐfinissait des rÃīles spÃĐcifiques pour chaque membre de lâÃĐquipage et proposait des amÃĐliorations techniques destinÃĐes Ã  accroÃŪtre la manÅuvrabilitÃĐ et lâefficacitÃĐ au combat.

history:
Missiessy was a French naval officer promoted to the rank of rear admiral in 1793. Like many officers during the Terror, he temporarily left France before returning to service. On his return, he was entrusted by Jean-Charles de Borda with assisting in the introduction of the metric system in French ports and naval arsenals, and he played a key role in the standardisation of coastal signals, contributing to safer navigation and improved maritime communication.
Between 1804 and 1805, Missiessy commanded the Rochefort squadron during British blockades in the West Indies, successfully supplying French colonies and disrupting British operations without losing a ship. His mission formed part of a broader diversionary strategy linked to Napoleonâs planned invasion of England, though the lack of full strategic disclosure limited coordination with other French squadrons.
Missiessy was also recognised for his influential treatise on the fitting-out and internal organisation of warships, published in 1797. Drawing on the model of a 74-gun vessel, the work promoted standardised layouts, clarified the functions of shipboard spaces, defined precise roles for crew members, and proposed technical improvements to enhance manoeuvrability and combat readiness. 
For all these reasons, Missiessy appears to have been held in high esteem by Freycinet.

Autour de lui se forma une gÃĐnÃĐration appelÃĐe Ã  marquer durablement la mÃĐdecine franÃ§aise, parmi laquelle Jean-Nicolas Corvisart, Pierre-FranÃ§ois Percy et Dominique Jean Larrey. history: Pierre-Joseph Desault was a surgeon who introduced an innovative method of teaching anatomy based on the systematic study of human corpses. As chief surgeon at the HÃītel-Dieu in Paris, he transformed surgical training by combining lectures and close supervision with live clinical practice, teaching while performing surgical procedures. Desault trained many of the most renowned surgeons of his time, notably Jean-Nicolas Corvisart, Pierre-FranÃ§ois Percy, and Dominique Jean Larrey. The political violence and radicalism of the Terror took a severe toll on his own health, contributing to his premature death.

Lorsque PÃĐron entreprit, en 1805, son travail de nomenclature, Jaubert avait reÃ§u mission dâÃĐtablir une alliance avec le chah de Perse, FatáļĨ-Ali Shah QÄjÄr, dirigÃĐe contre lâAngleterre et la Russie. Toutefois, la paix conclue par NapolÃĐon avec Alexandre Ier de Russie le 7 juillet 1807 â signÃĐe sur un radeau au milieu du NiÃĐmen â priva le traitÃĐ de Finkenstein dâune grande part de sa portÃĐe politique.
history: Jaubert studied Turkish, Arabic, and Persian languages at the Ãcole des Langues Orientales in Paris, was appointed interpreter to the French legation in Constantinople before being assigned as interpreter to NapolÃĐonâs expedition to Egypt (1798-99) and in 1799, became senior interpreter in the service of NapolÃĐon. When PÃĐron started working at the nomenclature, in 1805, Jaubert was entrusted with the establishment of an alliance with the Persian Shah FatáļĨ-Ali Shah QÄjÄr directed against England and Russia. On his way to Persia, he was made prisoner by the Ottomans and was held for eight months. He was finally received by the Shah at an audience in Tehran in June 1806. The negotiations were quite successful and led to the Treaty of Finkenstein, which was signed on 4 May 1807. This service to the nation probably led PÃĐron to include Jaubert in his nomenclature. La Relation du voyage dans la mer du Sud de FrÃĐzier (voir lâillustration dans "en savoir plus") constitua une source largement consultÃĐe par les navigateurs, parmi lesquels Bougainville (voir la notice Baie Bougainville en Australie-MÃĐridionale) et La PÃĐrouse ; lâouvrage fut dâailleurs traduit en anglais. Les descriptions prÃĐcises, les analyses approfondies et lâabondante illustration qui caractÃĐrisent les publications de FrÃĐzier furent particuliÃĻrement apprÃĐciÃĐes des lecteurs intÃĐressÃĐs par la botanique et lâhistoire naturelle, Ã  lâimage de PÃĐron. history: FrÃĐzier's  Relation du voyage dans la mer du Sud (see the illustration in Details), was a source widely consulted by navigators, including Bougainville (see the notice Baie Bougainville* in SA) and La PÃĐrouse, and was translated into English. It was also a bestseller bought by philosophers like Montesquieu* and voyageurs. The detailed descriptions, analysis, and illustrations of FrÃĐzierâs publications were particularly appreciated by botanical or natural history readers like PÃĐron. FrÃĐzier describes the native plant Fragaria chiloensis (beach strawberry) from the island of Chiloe in Chili which he introduced to France where it was hybridised and became the strawberry we now eat.

Duhamel possÃĐdait une connaissance approfondie des arbres, de la sylviculture, de la culture des plantes et de la botanique ; il sâintÃĐressa ÃĐgalement Ã  la formation du corps des ingÃĐnieurs et officiers de la construction navale, ainsi quâÃ  la santÃĐ des ÃĐquipages.

history: In this position, he studied the various problems that faced the French Navy during the 18th century: those linked to shipbuilding, and the need to use timber resistant to water and worms, and basic materials like hemp. Duhamel had extensive knowledge and expertise about trees, sylviculture, plant cultivation and botany, the training of the corps of shipbuilding engineers and officers, and the health of crews. Accordingly, Duhamel created a school to train future shipbuilders (see Details).

Lagrange Bay is a coastal indentation located in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, south of Broome. The surrounding landscape is largely flat and low-lying, typical of the sandy and gently elevated shorelines that characterise this part of the north-western Australian coast.
The name honours the mathematician Giuseppe Lodovico Lagrangia, better known as Joseph-Louis Lagrange (1736â1813). Born in Turin, then the capital of the Kingdom of Sardinia, Lagrange later became associated with France and was regarded as a French citizen â a status that also corresponded to his own wishes, as the paternal branch of his family was of French origin. One of the great scientific figures of the Enlightenment, Lagrange made fundamental contributions to mechanics and mathematical analysis.

history:
In 1802, NapolÃĐon Bonaparte appointed him Captain-General of Martinique and Saint Lucia, positions that formed part of the French colonial system in the Caribbean and supported the logistical and military framework of the Saint-Domingue expedition.
The Saint-Domingue expedition, to which this designation refers, formed part of Franceâs attempt to reassert control over the colony in 1802. Despite the rapid capture of several ports and a number of military successes, French forces encountered sustained resistance led by Toussaint Louverture and his lieutenants in the islandâs mountainous interior. Engagements, particularly around CrÃŠte-Ã -Pierrot, proved especially costly. In a context of guerrilla warfare, supply shortages, and the exhaustion of troops, the expedition was progressively weakened. The arrest of Toussaint Louverture in June 1802 did not suffice to restore lasting French control.

Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes: âAfter Cape Villaret, the land trends eastward, forming an indentation eight leagues wide, which we did not enter; Cape Huygens marks its northern extremity.â

Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes: âNear the sea, the ground consists of white sand dunes and cliffs of vividly coloured red rock; a continuous reef, awash at the surface, extends along most of the coastline, though without projecting far offshore. It is at the point where the reef begins to the south that the reddish cliffs we have just mentioned also commence; they extend uninterrupted as far as Coulomb Point, beyond which the dunes reappear.

This description corresponds closely to the present-day morphology of Coulomb Point, which is primarily composed of sandstone whose characteristic red colour results from iron oxides formed through the weathering and oxidation of iron-bearing minerals within the rock matrix.

The proximity of Coulomb Point and Cape Bertholet within the nomenclature is unlikely to be coincidental. history:
The attribution of this toponym forms part of a system of naming closely connected to the scholarly networks of the Institut National des Sciences et des Arts (now the Institut de France), of which Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot was one of the leading figures.
Like Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, Carnot applied mathematics to the study of physics. His work in geometry proved particularly valuable to engineer-geographersâespecially those of the Baudin expeditionâas well as to naval officers such as Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, insofar as it went beyond Euclidean geometryâcentred on the study of shapes and figuresâto develop more general approaches and demonstrations that could be applied in surveying and field measurement operations.

characteristic: The Lacepede Islands are a group of small, low-lying atolls composed of coarse sand and limestone, resting on a broad fringing reef platform. The reefs, forming four principal islands, were named after the naturalist Bernard Germain de LacÃĐpÃĻde (see Baie LacÃĐpÃĻde* in South Australia). They are of major ecological significance, supporting one of the largest colonies of brown boobies and the largest breeding population of lesser frigatebirds in the Indian Ocean, as well as serving as an important nesting site for green turtles. Uninhabited today, the islands were exploited for guano in the late nineteenth century and are now protected and managed for their environmental value.

history:
The reefs proved hazardous to the Casuarina, commanded by Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, when, on 10 April 1803, it was carried by the currents into a tide race near these shoals. 
Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet also noted the presence of âconsiderable flocks of seabirdsâ off the coasts of these islands. He writes in his account: âWhen we passed (in April 1803) near the Lacepede Islands, at sunrise we observed large flocks of seabirds leaving the land and flying towards the west and west-northwest. Their numbers were so great that one might have thought a complete migration of the birds of the region was taking place. From about three in the afternoon until sunset, they returned to the land, which led us to suppose that there were additional shoals offshore from the Lacepede Islands, and that these shoals provided abundant food for the birds that frequented them.â (pp. 239â240)

Nicolas Baudin notes in his sea journal on 26 July 1801: âIn the evening, shortly before sunset, we observed a large number of whales, which appeared to us much larger than those seen off Terre dâEndracht.
