characteristic:
Cape Borda is a headland located north of Broome, on the western coast of the Dampier Peninsula, which forms a significant environmental transition zone between the Great Sandy Desert to the south and the monsoonal tropics to the north.
Similarly to Emeriau, the name Borda has also been applied to a geological formation, the Borda Sandstone, which is slightly younger than the Emeriau formation (c. 65 million years old). This unit, recognised only at Cape Borda, is composed of lateritised fluvial sands and gravels.
Jean-Charles de Borda (1733â1799), known as the chevalier de Borda, was a French mathematician, physicist, and naval officer.

It has been named either after Louis Marie Maximilien de Caffarelli du Falga (1756â1799), known as Maximilien Caffarelli, a French general admired for his bravery and celebrated in his time as a national hero (see: Cap Caffarelli, South Australia), or after his younger brother, Louis Marie Joseph Maximilien FranÃ§ois Auguste Caffarelli du Falga (1766â1849), who, like Maximilien, was also a distinguished general, a Knight of the Legion of Honour, and held important ministerial responsibilities.

history:
In volume II of the voyage account, Freycinet corrected a naming error made by PÃĐron in the first volume of the historical narrative, where Caffarelli Island had mistakenly been designated as Berthoud Bay. As the charts had not yet been completed when that first account was published, the mistake had not yet been demonstrated; it was rectified by Freycinet in this second volume, where he explains that âon the 15th [April 1803], shortly after we were under sail, land was sighted; we recognised it as an island two leagues in length, which was named Caffarelli Island.â
The name Caffarelli appears twice in South Australia and once in Western Australia, indicating the importance attached to this heroic figure, who was regarded as a model of patriotic sacrifice.

Adrien-Hubert BruÃĐ, a very young sailor associated with the expedition, was found aboard the Naturaliste on 19 October 1800, after it had already sailed, and was then entered on the rolls as a first-class novice. On y a recensÃĐ 225 espÃĻces de coraux durs dans la zone intertidale. La rÃĐgion est soumise Ã  de fortes amplitudes de marÃĐe, de lâordre de 8 mÃĻtres, ainsi quâÃ  une forte turbiditÃĐ. Elle est ÃĐgalement influencÃĐe par des courants de plus grande ÃĐchelle, notamment lâIndonesian Throughflow via la partie interne du plateau nord-ouest. Lâensemble est reconnu comme une zone importante pour les requins et les raies, abritant des espÃĻces menacÃĐes ainsi que des habitats reproductifs majeurs.
PÃĐron donna Ã  cet archipel le nom de Bonaparte ÂŦ en lâhonneur du premier magistrat de notre nation et auguste protecteur de notre expÃĐdition Âŧ, en rÃĐfÃĐrence Ã  NapolÃĐon Bonaparte, alors Premier Consul du gouvernement franÃ§ais. characteristic:
The Bonaparte Archipelago is located in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, within the Countries of the Balanggarra, Wunambal Gaambera, Ngarinyin, and Miriuwung Gajerrong peoples.
The archipelago comprises several hundred islands and islets lying offshore along the stretch of coast between Collier Bay to the southwest and Admiralty Gulf to the northeast, including islands within Admiralty Gulf itself. Most are small islands, islets, or emergent rocks, and the area also includes several submerged banks and shoals.
Its coastal and marine habitats are characterised by reefs, mangroves, seagrass meadows, and intertidal mud and sand flats. A total of 225 species of hard corals have been recorded in the intertidal zone. The region is subject to large tidal fluctuations, with tidal ranges of around 8 metres, and to high turbidity. It is also influenced by broader-scale currents, including the Indonesian Throughflow via the inner North-West Shelf. The area is recognised as an Important Shark and Ray Area, supporting threatened species as well as key reproductive habitats.
PÃĐron named this archipelago âin honour of the first magistrate of our nation and august protector of our expeditionâ, NapolÃĐon Bonaparte, in reference to the period when he was First Consul of the French government. The name thus reflects the political context in which Bonaparte sponsored the voyage of discovery to the Southern Lands (see also Golfe Bonaparte* in South Australia).

history:
Freycinet warns: âThe number of reefs that extend throughout the Bonaparte Archipelago, together with the considerable number of islands and islets found there, makes navigation in these waters difficult and even dangerous. As all the reefs are awash, the sounding line cannot indicate their proximity; this would be a very serious inconvenience at night, were it not for the ease with which one can anchor almost everywhere, allowing the navigator each evening to place himself out of danger. In some places the reefs extend far offshore, whereas elsewhere they lie close to the coast. Our chart indicates a great many of them; however, one must not suppose that they have all been marked. Especially between the islands, we have reason to believe that there are many banks that we did not examine. Thus, until a complete survey of these waters has been carried out, it is advisable to proceed with the utmost caution.â (*Navigation et GÃĐographie*, p. 248).
For his part, PÃĐron states: âUnder the general designation of Bonaparte Archipelago (chart no. 27), we have included the long succession of islands and islets extending from the Champagny Islands in the west to the Institut Islands, over a distance of about 60 nautical leagues. This vast ensemble is made up of different groups, which have been designated by the names of Champagny Islands, Arcole Islands, Maret Islands, Montalivet Islands, and Institut Islandsâ (PÃĐron, vol. I, p. 140). See the next entries.

characteristic:
The Champagny Islands are located off the coast of the Kimberley region, in the southern part of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and form part of the Camden Sound Marine Park, known to the Traditional Owners as Nimenba. Today, they are recognised for their rich bird biodiversity. The islands are also known turtle nesting sites (Green Turtles and Hawksbill Turtles, which are both listed as threatened or endangered) and are fringed by extensive intertidal reefs on both their seaward and landward sides, across a range of depths.
They are named after Jean-Baptiste de NompÃĻre de Champagny, duc de Cadore (1756â1834), who served as Minister of the Interior from 1804 to 1807, and then as Minister of Foreign Affairs from 1807 to 1811 (see the entry Port Champagny in South Australia).

They form a protected area within the Kimberley Marine Park and are characterised by rocky shorelines and fringing reefs exposed to strong tidal movements.
The group was named after the Battle of Arcole, fought between French and Austrian forces in 1796 (see the entry Pic dâArcole* in Tasmania), one of the most celebrated episodes of the Napoleonic legend. Its commemorative use reflects the powerful propaganda value attached to this battle, even though its historical reality was more complex than the heroic myth later suggested.

history:
Baudin asked his artists to sketch in colour the âbizarre and picturesqueâ forms of these islands, which were later included in the historical atlas of the expedition. 
PÃĐron even wrote that several of them resembled âimmense ancient tombs.â His narrative also shows that the expedition observed and documented the very large tidal ranges characteristic of the Kimberley waters, which are among the greatest in the world. He notes: âDuring the time we remained at anchor among the DâArcole Islands, the difference in soundings showed us that the tide rises here by 20 to 25 feet; an observation that seems to confirm that of M. Ronsard on Depuch Island, and which itself agrees with that of Dampier. As is well known, that celebrated navigator nearly fell victim to these extraordinary tides, his vessel having been left dry in the very place where, on the previous day, he had anchored in 5 fathoms. This circumstance greatly increases the danger of navigation in these seas, and appears to be the principal cause of the violent currents felt there.â

characteristic:
Duguesclin Island is a remote and uninhabited island, situated along a rugged stretch of coast marked by strong tidal movements and by the presence of ancient Indigenous rock-art sites.
It is named after Bertrand Du Guesclin (c. 1320â1380), also spelled Duguesclin, a knight and military leader associated with what nineteenth-century historiography came to designate as the **Hundred Yearsâ War**. This long cycle of conflicts, interrupted by truces, opposed the Plantagenet kingdom of England and the Valois kingdom of France from 1337 to 1453. The inclusion of Duguesclinâs name in the voyageâs nomenclature thus linked this Australian coastline to a long French military memory, predating the Napoleonic era by several centuries yet still available for the symbolic construction of the Southern Lands by the French.

Âŧ Cette parole souligne symboliquement lâattachement que lui portait le royaume de France tout entier, jusque dans ses couches les plus modestes. Charles V (1338-1380), roi de France, le nomma connÃĐtable de France en 1370, faisant de lui le chef des armÃĐes royales. NapolÃĐon admira en lui Ã  la fois la tÃĐnacitÃĐ guerriÃĻre, la reconquÃŠte mÃĐthodique des territoires occupÃĐs par les Anglais et lâusage dâune stratÃĐgie dâusure voisine de la politique de la terre brÃŧlÃĐe. Lâironie de lâhistoire veut toutefois que lâEmpereur nâait pas su prÃĐvoir que les Russes recourraient contre lui, lors de la campagne de 1812, Ã  des procÃĐdÃĐs comparables.
history: Bertrand du Guesclin is most commonly associated with the following saying: âCourage grants what beauty denies.â The phrase encapsulates the traditional image of a man reputed to be physically ungainly, yet whose bravery, endurance, and military abilities made him one of the great French heroes of the Hundred Yearsâ War. Legend also relates that, after being taken prisoner in Spain by the Prince of Wales following the Battle of NÃĄjera on 3 April 1367, and while the terms of his ransom were being discussed, he declared: âMy lord, the King of Castile will pay one half, and the King of France the rest; and if that should not suffice, there is not a spinner in France who can spin who would not earn my ransom by spinning.â The remark symbolically suggests the depth of support he enjoyed throughout the kingdom of France, even among its humblest people. King Charles V of France (1338-1380) appointed him Constable of France in 1370, thus making him commander of the royal armies. Napoleon admired in him both his martial tenacity, his methodical reconquest of territories held by the English, and his use of an attritional strategy akin to scorched-earth warfare. Yet history carries a certain irony, for the Emperor failed to foresee that the Russians would employ comparable methods against him during the campaign of 1812.

history:
With a certain irritation toward the officer Ronsard, Freycinet draws attention to âa curious, though isolated, observationâ made on 11 August 1801 near Colbert Island: âthis observation gave us 4 fathoms for the greatest rise of the water on the day after the new moon; it is unfortunate that the exact moment of high tide could not be determined. M. Ronsard, to whom these results are due, merely states that the reversal of the current took place at 7.20 in the morning, but that by that time the sea had already risen by one or two feet.â
This remark underscores the importance Freycinet attached to the precise measurement of tides in these waters, where the magnitude of the tidal range determined that of the tidal currents and, consequently, the conditions of navigation. The moment of high tide, understood as the exact instant when the water reaches its maximum height before the ebb begins, was for him an essential datum.
Ronsard noted only in his journal: âOn 23 Thermidor [Year IX, 11 August 1801] in the morning, an attempt was made to land on islands situated at 14Â°48' and 122Â°12'; but the boat returned without having been able to find a landing place.â He also recorded in his nautical journal: âAt 7 oâclock the tide was low, in 20 fathoms of water; the reversal took place at 7.20; the depth had already increased by about one foot.â

history:
Freycinet states that he relied on the observational tables established by the astronomer-mathematicians Bouguer and La Caille in order to determine the time at which high tide should occur at the moment of the new and full moon. He also mentions the method proposed by Bernoulli, though he considered it less convenient to use: âBernouilli has given one whose form is different and whose use is less convenient; perhaps it is more exact in theory, but in practice such extreme exactness is still far from being found in observations.â
This remark is revealing of the importance Freycinet attached to the gap between the elegance of mathematical constructions and the concrete constraints of navigation, where the precision of calculation constantly had to contend with the uncertainty of measurements taken at sea.

history: To understand why PÃĐron chose to include the abbÃĐ Mably in the official nomenclature of the voyage, one must probably look less for the direct influence of a specifically acknowledged author than for the presence of a shared intellectual horizon, deeply rooted in the political culture of the revolutionary generation. Like many young republicans formed in the 1790s, PÃĐron seems to have been receptive to themes that Mably had helped to disseminate: the exaltation of civic virtue, the critique of inequalities and social corruption, and the primacy of the common good over private interests. What is at stake here is less an explicit filiation than a shared moral and political language, which helps to explain the tension in PÃĐronâs thought between admiration for certain forms of natural simplicity and the ambition to refound, through science and observation, a form of knowledge useful to the nation. Marked by rugged relief and fragmented pockets of rainforest, it forms part of a biodiverse coastal and marine region free of introduced mammals and supporting native fauna, including nesting flatback turtles. The island also holds cultural significance for the Wunambal Gaambera people, for whom it is a traditional site for harvesting marine resources, notably flatback turtles, and is managed within their Uunguu Coast zone.

Pour toutes ces raisons, le choix des noms doit ÃŠtre compris non comme une simple juxtaposition de rÃĐfÃĐrences savantes, mais dans le contexte des rÃĐseaux intellectuels, institutionnels et personnels qui reliaient les figures ainsi commÃĐmorÃĐes.
history:

More broadly, he developed an analytical method based on the description and comparison of morphological characters, enabling species to be distinguished through increasingly precise differentiations. He became convinced that organisms could change over time, in opposition to Cuvier, who defended the fixity of species.
PÃĐron appears to have been receptive to Lamarckâs transformist ideas and, in several passages, adopted positions that implicitly distanced him from Cuvierâs fixism, even if this remained discreet, since Cuvier was one of his principal patrons. For these reasons, the choice of names should be understood not as a simple list of learned references, but in the context of the intellectual, institutional, and personal networks linking the figures they commemorate.

characteristic:
This group of islands lies off the Kimberley coast, in the northern sector of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and now forms part of the North Kimberley Marine Park. It is notable for its rich marine environments: more than 210 species of hard coral have been recorded on the Maret Islands alone (Richards et al. 2015). The archipelago also includes a highly diverse mangrove system, together with important dugong hotspots extending from the Maret Islands to the Montalivet Islands.
The Maret Islands take their name from Hugues-Bernard Maret, Secretary of State (see Maret Bay in South Australia). The principal island of the group likewise bears the name Maret Island.
history:
Maret was honoured by having a group of islands named after him because, in his capacity as Minister-Secretary of State, he stood at the very centre of the imperial regime. He served as the indispensable intermediary through whom passed discussions, correspondence, projects, as well as the work and petitions of the other ministers. Maret also appears to have exercised a significant influence over appointments. In the draft reports and memoranda he submitted to the Emperor, lists of candidates for particular offices were attached, and NapolÃĐon often left the choice of nomination to him.

history:
PÃĐron was particularly indebted to Jussieu, whose support played a decisive role in securing his embarkation on the expedition to the Southern Lands, as Deleuze explains in his Historical Eulogy of FranÃ§ois PÃĐron:
âThe French Government had ordered an expedition to the Southern Lands [...] PÃĐron asked to be employed on it; but as the number of savants was already complete, he was at first unable to gain acceptance. He turned to M. de Jussieu, one of the commissioners responsible for selecting the naturalists, who advised him to write a memorandum setting out his reasons. Jussieu then reported to his colleagues on the conversation he had had with PÃĐron and, together with M. de LacepÃĻde, persuaded them not to reject a young man who combined extraordinary ardour with a breadth of knowledge quite rare at his age. A few days later, PÃĐron read before the Institut a memoir on the usefulness of adding to the other savants of the expedition a physician-naturalist specifically charged with conducting research in anthropology, or the history of man; he won all the votes [...]â

La lÃĐgende de Guillaume Tell, devenue lâun des ÃĐlÃĐments marquants de la mÃĐmoire nationale suisse, fut remise en valeur pendant la RÃĐvolution franÃ§aise et rÃĐinterprÃĐtÃĐe comme un emblÃĻme de la rÃĐsistance Ã  la tyrannie, en particulier Ã  celle que lâon associait aux monarchies dynastiques de lâEurope. Cette appropriation politique et morale trouva aussi une expression musicale lorsque AndrÃĐ GrÃĐtry composa Ã  Paris, en 1791, un opÃĐra-comique sur ce sujet, Åuvre que NapolÃĐon admirait beaucoup. Durant la pÃĐriode rÃĐvolutionnaire, un vaisseau de quatre-vingts canons de la marine franÃ§aise porta ÃĐgalement le nom de Guillaume Tell. history: The name Guillaume-Tell [William Tell] in the nomenclature invokes the figure of the patriotic hero who, in tradition, fought for the independence of his native Switzerland against Habsburg authority in the fourteenth century. The legend of William Tell, which became an important element of Swiss national memory, was revived during the French Revolution and reinterpreted as an emblem of resistance to tyranny, particularly that associated with the dynastic monarchies of Europe. This political and moral appropriation also found expression in music, when AndrÃĐ GrÃĐtry composed a comic opera on the subject in Paris in 1791, a work much admired by NapolÃĐon. During the revolutionary period, an eighty-gun ship of the French Navy likewise bore the name Guillaume Tell. Captured by the British in 1800 after the Battle of the Nile, during the Egyptian Campaign (see glossary), the vessel further illustrates the circulation of this heroic name within the military and political culture of the age.

characteristic:
Today, of the group of islands named Montalivet by the French, only the two largest still retain the name Montalivet. West Montalivet is a volcanic island capped with laterite. Reached by canoe or raft, it served as a wet-season occupation site and as a hunting ground for mangguru (turtle). During the Second World War, the island was occupied by the men of the 344 Radar Station. Its intertidal zone displays a remarkable diversity of coral species, although Freycinet was only able to observe the reef indirectly, through the surf breaking over it.
The island takes its name from Jean-Pierre Bachasson, comte de Montalivet (1766â1823), a senior administrator who oversaw major transport infrastructure works that contributed to the modernisation of France and whose name was given to two places in this nomenclature; see also Cape Montalivet.

Malus Island is located in the north-eastern corner of Whalers Bay, within the Dampier Archipelago, off the north-west coast of Western Australia. It is characterised by a naturally sheltered cove with a sandy bottom, providing a safe anchorage. This geographical configuration favoured the establishment, in the nineteenth century, of a shore-based whaling station, the remains of which are situated on the elevated ground overlooking the cove. 
The island was named by Louis-Claude de Freycinet in honour of the French engineer, physicist and mathematician Ãtienne Louis Malus (1775â1812).
history_fr:

Dans ce document cartographique, les traits pleins correspondent aux contours ÃĐtablis Ã  partir dâobservations directes, tandis que les tracÃĐs en pointillÃĐs signalent les parties conjecturales des ÃŪles qui nâont pas fait lâobjet de relevÃĐs prÃĐcis.
Il est probable que Freycinet ait rencontrÃĐ Ãtienne-Louis Malus Ã  Anvers en 1804, Ã  lâÃĐpoque oÃđ ce dernier ÃĐtait chargÃĐ des travaux dâextension du port, tandis que Freycinet commandait le brick Le Voltigeur. history:
Freycinet notes that Malus Island is smaller than Rosemary Island. He also presents a specific plan of these two islands in an inset accompanying the Map of a Part of Terre de Witt. In this cartographic document, he uses solid lines to indicate the outlines recorded through direct observation, while dotted lines mark the conjectural traces of the remaining portions of the islands that were not directly surveyed.
It is likely that Freycinet met Ãtienne-Louis Malus in Antwerp in 1804, when Malus was overseeing port expansion works and Freycinet was serving as commander of the brig Le Voltigeur.
